narration: Define a void function named showBalance that takes a double parameter named balance.
Define a function named deposit that returns a double and takes no parameters.
Define a function named withdraw that returns a double and takes a double parameter named balance.
In the main function, declare a double variable named balance and initialize it to zero.
Declare an integer variable named choice and initialize it to zero.
Display a menu prompt using standard output with options: 1 for show balance, 2 for deposit, 3 for withdrawal, and 4 for exit.
Accept user input into the choice variable using standard input.
Create a switch statement that evaluates the choice variable.
In case 1, invoke the showBalance function passing the balance variable, then break.
In case 2, update the balance by adding the return value of the deposit function using the plus-equals operator, then break.
In case 3, update the balance by subtracting the return value of the withdraw function using the minus-equals operator, then break.
In case 4, display a thank you message using standard output and break.
In the default case, display an invalid choice message using standard output.
Wrap the menu and switch logic inside a do-while loop that continues while choice is not equal to 4.
Implement the showBalance function to display the balance with a dollar sign and a newline.
Include the iomanip header file to enable floating-point formatting.
Use std::setprecision(2) and std::fixed in the output stream to display the balance with two decimal places.
Implement the deposit function by declaring a local double variable named amount and initializing it to zero.
Prompt the user to enter the amount to be deposited and read the input into the amount variable.
Add an if statement to check if amount is greater than zero; if true, return amount, otherwise print an error message and return zero.
Implement the withdraw function by declaring a local double variable named amount.
Prompt the user to enter the amount to be withdrawn and read the input into the amount variable.
Add an if statement to check if amount is greater than the current balance; if true, print insufficient funds and return zero.
Add an else-if statement to check if amount is less than zero; if true, print an invalid amount message and return zero.
In the else block of the withdrawal logic, return the amount to be withdrawn.
After reading the choice input, call std::cin.clear() to reset error flags.
Call std::cin.ignore() or std::cin.flush() to clear the input buffer and remove leftover newline characters.
